text: The surgeon sweeps the omentum upward, revealing the uterus in the surgical field.
The surgeon reveals the right ovary, which is visible adjacent to the uterus after sweeping the omentum upwards.
The surgeon gently pushes the small intestine above the sacral promontory to maintain a clear view of the surgical field.
The iliac vessels are visible in the surgical field, with surrounding anatomical structures such as the uterus and omentum previously swept aside.
The surgeon exposes the cecum and the tip of the appendix, which are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon exposes the triangle of dome, a region likely involving the peritoneal cavity, with visible anatomical landmarks such as the cecum, appendix, iliac vessels, and ovary in the surrounding area.
The infundibulopelvic ligament is visible, with the ureter seen deep below in the surgical field.
The surgeon displays the left side of the fimbrial end and ovary, which appear normal in appearance.
The fimbrial end of the left fallopian tube is visible, showing its anatomical position adjacent to the ovary.
The surgeon observes a distended sigmoid colon positioned in front of the surgical field.

The utero-sacral ligament and cul-de-sac are visible, located approximately 2.5 centimeters lateral to the utero-sacral ligament. The ureter is also visible in this area. The cul-de-sac appears normal, with no signs of endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), or free fluid. The ovarian fossa is also visible.

The video shows the ureter crossing laterally, with a view of the anterior and posterior cul-de-sac, ovary, and fallopian tube. The posterior cul-de-sac is visible, and the sigmoid colon is retracted to the left.

The camera view shows the peritoneum with the ascending colon, liver, gallbladder, and a few drops of trickled blood visible. The left lobe of the liver, stomach, diaphragm, and omentum covering the spleen are also observed before the view returns to the pelvis.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, applying methylene blue dye to assess tubal patency. The dye dribbles from both the left and right fallopian tubes, indicating that both tubes are open and patent.
The surgeon observes the methylene blue dye coming from both sides, indicating tubal patency, and proceeds to wash the area with saline.

The surgeon concludes the procedure by suctioning the dribbled methylene blue dye from the surgical field, ensuring all collected dye is removed. A small amount of saline is introduced from the cervix to clear the area.

The surgeon introduces suction irrigation to remove remaining methylene blue dye from the pelvic cavity, revealing a normal uterus, normal fallopian tubes, and healthy ovaries with no visible pathology.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical field, ensuring all remaining methylene blue dye is suctioned out. The laparoscopic telescope is removed, and the two 5mm ports used during the procedure do not require closure.
